coredata.dc:title: Electrochemical-thermal behaviors of retired power lithium-ion batteries during high-temperature and overcharge/over-discharge cycles
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 The Authors. Published by Elsevier Ltd.
coredata.prism:publisher: The Authors. Published by Elsevier Ltd.
coredata.dc:creator: Li, Caisheng
Liu, Xianqing
Wang, Changhong
Ye, Lisheng
Wu, Tingting
Liang, Zhixuan
Zhang, Zejie
Zeng, Ying
Li, Kaizhe
coredata.dc:description: To safely utilize retired power lithium-ion batteries (PLIBs) for secondary, LiFePO₄/graphite batteries were taken as research object. The electrochemical-thermal behaviors were investigated under different operating conditions. The experimental results show that heat generated will greatly increase, and the uneven distribution of temperature within the battery will become more severe during high-temperature cycles. Compared with room temperature cycling, the decay rate of battery SOH increased by 419.88 % after 400 cycles at high-temperature environment. After 400 cycles at 25 °C and 55 °C, the maximum temperature difference for 3C increased from 2.05 °C to 2.91 °C and 2.14 °C–3.03 °C, respectively, corresponding to the increases of 41.95 % and 41.59 %. After 800 cycles of overcharging, the battery state of health (SOH) was only 67.52 %; compared with the normal cycles, the decay rate increased by 156.25 %. Finally, a method for assessing the battery's health status based on the frequency of the peak area in the probability density function (PDF) was proposed. These results proved the effectiveness of the proposed method for evaluating the health of retired PLIBs.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Retired power lithium-ion batteries
Aging mechanism
Temperature characteristics
State of health assessment

The effect of overcharge and over-discharge for electrochemical performance
Heat generation behavior under overcharge and over-discharge cycles

Health status assessment under overcharge and over-discharge cycling

The specific peaks shift in the probability density curve after overcharge cycles and over-discharge cycles.

head.ce:title: Electrochemical-thermal behaviors of retired power lithium-ion batteries during high-temperature and overcharge/over-discharge cycles

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Guangdong University of Technology, Guangzhou, 510006, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Guangdong University of Technology, Guangzhou, 510006, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Jieyang Branch of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering Guangdong Laboratory (Rongjiang Laboratory), Jieyang, 515200, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Jieyang Branch of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering Guangdong Laboratory (Rongjiang Laboratory)

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Jieyang Branch of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering Guangdong Laboratory (Rongjiang Laboratory), Jieyang, 515200, China

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: These authors contributed equally to this work and should be considered co-first authors.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: To safely utilize retired power lithium-ion batteries (PLIBs) for secondary, LiFePO₄/graphite batteries were taken as research object. The electrochemical-thermal behaviors were investigated under different operating conditions. The experimental results show that heat generated will greatly increase, and the uneven distribution of temperature within the battery will become more severe during high-temperature cycles. Compared with room temperature cycling, the decay rate of battery SOH increased by 419.88 % after 400 cycles at high-temperature environment. After 400 cycles at 25 °C and 55 °C, the maximum temperature difference for 3C increased from 2.05 °C to 2.91 °C and 2.14 °C–3.03 °C, respectively, corresponding to the increases of 41.95 % and 41.59 %. After 800 cycles of overcharging, the battery state of health (SOH) was only 67.52 %; compared with the normal cycles, the decay rate increased by 156.25 %. Finally, a method for assessing the battery's health status based on the frequency of the peak area in the probability density function (PDF) was proposed. These results proved the effectiveness of the proposed method for evaluating the health of retired PLIBs.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: The data that has been used is confidential.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Nomenclature

ce:section.ce:para: Lithium-ion batteries are widely used in electric vehicles and hybrid electric vehicles due to their high energy density, long cycle life, rapid charging and discharging, and environmental friendliness [1–4]. In 2020, global electric vehicle sales reached 3.095 million units, and it is expected that the sales will reach 10 million units in 2025, 28 million units in 2030, and 56 million units in 2040 [5]. However, the service life of PLIBs typically ranges from 10 to 15 years, and they need to be retired when their capacity degrades below 80 % [6]. The large-scale retirement of lithium-ion batteries used in electric and hybrid vehicles offers significant social, economic, and environmental benefits [7,8]. Therefore, the efficient and environmentally friendly disposal of retired PLIBs is an urgent issue.

Retired PLIBs are typically processed through either cascading utilization or dismantling and recycling. As retired PLIBs still retain 80 % of their initial capacity, they can be repurposed for cascading uses, such as energy storage for smart grids, solar street lights, and other small-scale devices. Therefore, direct dismantling and recycling could result in a waste of energy. However, retired PLIBs exhibit significant variations in their electrochemical performance, particularly in terms of capacity, internal resistance, and state of charge (SOC) [9]. Consequently, diagnosing the degree of aging and assessing their health status is crucial before repurposing these batteries for secondary use.

To address the issues above, extensive research has been conducted by researchers. Gao et al. [10] combined the pseudo two-dimensional model with a genetic algorithm to demonstrate the effectiveness of screening retired PLIBs. Jiang et al. [6] combined non-destructive research with disassembly analysis to quantitatively study the aging mechanism of retired PLIBs. Liu et al. [11] used artificial neural networks and nonlinear autoregressive exogenous models to predict the aging process of PLIBs. Wang et al. [12] employed electrochemical impedance spectroscopy and instant integral voltage to estimate the consistency of the battery. Zhang et al. [13] proposed an electrochemical-thermal resolution model for properly analyzing the battery SOH. Chen et al. [14] put forward a full lifecycle, multi-stage, and multi-level battery health management model. Generally, battery SOH assessment methods can be classified into two categories: data-driven methods and model-driven methods [13]. Data-driven methods largely rely on accurate acquisition of raw data, most of which do not focus on the interpretation of aging mechanisms. Model-driven methods, composed of many nonlinear partial differential formulas and a set of electrochemical parameters, make it overly complicated and difficult to implement in practical applications.

Additionally, research indicates that overcharging, over-discharging, and temperature are the primary factors leading to the degradation of the electrochemical performance of PLIBs [15,16]. Overcharging can accelerate battery aging and in severe cases, lead to battery failure, thermal runaway, and explosion [17,18]. Liu et al. [19] found that even minor overcharging can hasten the aging process due to the loss of active material. Devie et al. [20] believe that overcharging can accelerate the degradation of electrochemical performance due to the presence of bubbles between the electrodes. Furthermore, the operating temperature of PLIBs should be maintained within the range of 23 °C–40 °C [21]. High-temperature environments can expedite the decline of battery capacity and may even lead to thermal runaway [22,23]. Zhao et al. [24] discovered that at an operating temperature of 55 °C and after 250 cycles, the battery capacity dropped to 80 %, while at 25 °C the same battery could cycle 3900 times. To keep the battery's temperature within an appropriate range, researchers have conducted extensive studies. Khaboshan et al. [25] investigated the battery thermal management system (BTMS) configurations with fins, metal foam, and phase change material (PCM) to minimize temperature of battery during 3C discharging. Kausthubharam et al. [26] presented a diagonal-type minichannel-based thermal management system for a 20 Ah pouch cell battery. Junran et al. [27] proposed sequence training and data shuffling means to improve long short-term memory battery voltage models. However, there's still limited research into the electrochemical performance degradation and heat generation mechanisms of retired PLIBs when cycled at high-temperature, overcharging, and over-discharging.

Based on the analysis above, this article uses the capacity, voltage, and internal resistance of retired PLIBs as screening factors and applies the K-means clustering algorithm for rapid selection and classification of these batteries. It quantitatively studies the degradation of the electrochemical performance and heat generation of retired PLIBs during overcharging, over-discharging, and high-temperature cycling. By utilizing PDF to process battery voltage-capacity data and based on the relationship between the peak frequency in the area of PDF curves and battery aging, a high-precision, efficient method for assessing battery health status has been proposed. The study investigates the impacts of overcharge cycles, over-discharge cycles, and high-temperature cycles on the aging mechanism and electrochemical-thermal behaviors of retired PLIBs to ensure their safety in secondary use.

The retired PLIBs from the Jiangsu Chunlan Group. Table 1 lists some essential parameters of the retired PLIBs. The power battery test system (IGBT-100V/300A-2) and constant temperature test chamber (−20–150 °C) are used to test the electrochemical performance of the battery. To begin with, test the battery capacity at a temperature of 25 °C by charging each battery to 3.65 V and discharging it to the cut-off voltage of 2.75 V, cycling three times. To ensure safety during the electrochemical testing process, the protection upper and lower cut-off voltages are set to 3.8 V and 2.6 V, respectively. C-rate is the charge or discharge current ratio to nominal capacity. The C-rate presented in this work is based on the cell initial capacity of 25 Ah. A constant current charge-discharge method was used at a rate of 1C (25A). The average discharge capacity from the second and third cycles was taken as the battery actual capacity. The internal resistance of retired PLIBs at different discharge depths was analyzed using hybrid pulse power characterization (HPPC) test, by applying a pulse current. The HPPC testing system and test steps are shown in Fig. 1 . It includes a power battery test system, a computer, and a constant temperature test chamber.

The K-means clustering algorithm is used to screen retired PLIBs, eliminating batteries that have aged significantly, and selecting batteries with similar capacities and internal resistances to ensure safety during testing. The K-means clustering algorithm belongs to dynamic clustering algorithms, which are easy to implement and have good clustering effects [28,29]. The specific steps are shown in Fig. 2 .

First, K initial centers are selected from the sample data, and all the data is divided into these K centers to form K initial clustering clusters. The remaining sample data are then classified into the nearest center according to the clustering center. After the completion of iteration calculations, the clustering center is continuously updated until the objective function reaches the set threshold. In the rapid screening of retired batteries, if there are n sample data sets
Χ = {
χ 1
,
χ 2
, . . .
χ n
)
,
Χ = {
χ 1
,
χ 2
, . . .
χ n
)
clusters are obtained after clustering. In the clustering criterion, the sum of squared errors is used as the clustering criterion, as shown in formula (1) [30].
(1)
J c
=
∑
j = 1
k

i = 1
n k

Where
m j
represents the mean of sample
χ j
, as shown in formula (2),
(2)
m j
=
1 n
∑
i = 1
n j
χ i
, i = 1 , 2 , 3 …
n j

The sum of squared errors criterion function is the sum of squared errors for all objects within their clusters relative to the cluster centers. A high accuracy in clustering results indicates a high degree of data similarity among the objects in the clusters.

Before performing clustering, calculate the similarity between battery capacity and internal resistance data.

is used to determine the degree of similarity between two sets of data; the larger the value of

. Furthermore, the distance function used to calculate the difference between two data sets must satisfy three conditions: non-negativity, symmetry, and triangle inequality, which are defined by formulas (3) to (5) [31],
(3)

Similarity measurement methods include Euclidean distance, Manhattan distance, etc., as shown in formulas (6) and (7) [32].

However, in the rapid screening and classification of retired PLIBs, it is necessary to assign weights to the distance formula based on the importance of data such as capacity, internal resistance, voltage, and the requirements of energy and power for the batteries in various cascaded utilization scenarios, as illustrated in formula (8). This approach makes the battery screening and classification results more accurate.
(8)

Subsequently, overcharge cycle, over-discharge cycle, and high-temperature cycle experiments were designed to investigate the electrochemical performance degradation mechanisms and temperature characteristics of retired PLIBs.

At room temperature, PLIBs exhibit slow performance degradation and a longer lifespan. However, in high-temperature environments, batteries generate severe heat and suffer structural damage, leading to a rapid degradation of battery performance. To further explore the aging characteristic of retired PLIBs at different temperatures, this article analyzes the electrochemical performance and heat generation characteristic of retired PLIBs during cycling at 25 °C and 55 °C to reveal their aging mechanism. The high-temperature aging and temperature test system for retired PLIBs is shown in Fig. 3 . It includes a power battery test system, Agilent (34901A), T-type thermocouples (Omega TT-T-30-SLE, temperature range of −267∼260 °C, accuracy of 1 %), a computer, and a constant temperature test chamber. Retired PLIBs are separately placed in constant temperature chambers at 25 °C and 55 °C.The power battery test system is used to test the electrochemical performance of the battery. The constant current charge and discharge method is used, with a charging rate of 1C and cut-off voltage of 3.65 V; the discharge rate is 3C, and the cut-off voltage is 2.75 V. To ensure safety during the electrochemical testing process, the protection upper and lower cut-off voltages are set to 3.8 V and 2.6 V, respectively. The Agilent and T-type thermocouples are used to collect temperature data during the discharge process, and the arrangement of 5 temperature measurement points is shown in Fig. 3. Then, the temperature data is processed to obtain the maximum temperature and the minimum temperature at any time of the discharge process, and the maximum temperature difference is calculated for studying the heat generation characteristics of the battery. After 100 cycles, place the battery in a 25 °C constant temperature chambers for 3 h, and then a test of the battery capacity and internal resistance is conducted.

Overcharging and over-discharging can lead to battery swelling, safety valve breakage, or even fires and explosions. To analyze the heat generation and thermal stability during overcharge cycles and over-discharge cycles. The working temperature is 25 °C, and after normal cycles, overcharging cycles, and over-discharging cycles, the battery is discharged at a constant current and temperature data is collected. During the overcharge cycles, the constant current charging current is 3C, and the cutoff voltage is 3.65 V. Then, a constant current of 0.1C (2.5 A) is used for charging for 0.5 h, resulting in a 5 % overcharge of the battery capacity (1.25 Ah). The constant current discharging current is 3C, and the cutoff voltage is 2.75 V.

During over-discharging cycles, the constant current charging rate is 3C with a cut-off voltage of 3.65 V; the constant current discharge rate is 3C with a cut-off voltage of 2.30 V. To ensure safety during the electrochemical testing process, the protection upper and lower cut-off voltages are set to 3.8 V and 2.6 V, respectively. Correspondingly, the Agilent and T-type thermocouples are used to monitor and record the battery temperature during the discharge process, as shown in Fig. 3. After 100 cycles, place the battery in a 25 °C constant temperature chambers for 3 h, and then a test of the battery capacity and internal resistance is performed to assess the impact of overcharge and over-discharge cycles on electrochemical-thermal behaviors.

Battery aging analysis methods mainly include the capacity increment method and differential voltage analysis method. Both of these methods are non-destructive analytical techniques that reveal the battery aging mechanism through the small current charge-discharge voltage curves. The core of the capacity increment analysis method is to differentiate the voltage-capacity curve of lithium-ion batteries, as shown in formula (9):
(9)
I C =
d Q
d V
Where
d Q
represents the change in battery capacity, and
d V
represents the change in voltage. The advantages of the incremental capacity analysis and differential voltage analysis are that they can transform the rather flat portions of the battery voltage-capacity curve into peaks and valleys in the
I C
and
d V
curves. After battery cycling, changes in the voltage-capacity curve will be reflected as changes in the
I C
curve peak values, peak positions, and peak area. These data can be extracted from the voltage-capacity curve to serve as factors for analyzing battery performance degradation. However, in practical applications, the voltage-capacity curve can be quite stable, which may lead to the phenomenon of
d V = 0
, consequently generating a substantial amount of noise.

The main errors during the experiment come from the directly measured parameters, which include temperature errors, current errors, and voltage errors. To reduce temperature errors, T-type thermocouples (Omega TT-T-30-SLE) are used to collect temperature data. The thermocouples test accuracy is 1 % in the temperature range of −267∼260 °C. The minimum surface temperature of battery is 25 °C, so the maximum relative error of the surface temperature is:
(10a)
ς
T ω
=
ς
T ω
T ω
=
[
260 −
(
− 267
)
]
× 1 %
25
= 0.21 %

According to the parameter introduction of battery test system (IGBT-100V/300A-2), the maximum current allowed by the equipment is 300A, and the test accuracy is 0.1 %. The minimum current used in this experiment is 25A, so the maximum relative error is:
(11)
ς I =
ς I
I
=
300 × 0.1 %
25
= 1.2 %

According to the parameter introduction of battery test system (IGBT-100V/300A-2), the maximum voltage allowed by the equipment is 100V, and the test accuracy is 0.1 %. The minimum voltage used in this experiment is 2.30V, so the maximum relative error is:
(12)
ς U =
ς U
U
=
100 × 0.1 %
2.30
= 4.35 %
To sum up, the measurement error of all parameters is less than 5 %, so the experimental data in this study are reliable.

First, the number of cluster centers needs to be determined. If too few cluster centers are chosen, it may lead to poor clustering performance; if too many cluster centers are used, it may result in the same data being classified into different categories. For the K-means clustering algorithm, it requires continuous adjustment of sample classification to acquire suitable cluster centers. This paper determines the number of cluster centers by calculating the sum of squared errors of samples under different cluster centers, as shown in formula (10).
(10b)
S =
∑
i = 1
k
∑
p ∈
C i
| p
−
m i
|
2
Here,
C i
is the i-th cluster,
p
is a sample in the cluster, and
m i
is the center of the cluster.

Then, using the K-means clustering algorithm, the retired PLIBs are sorted and classified based on their capacity, voltage, and internal resistance as the selection clustering factors. The 32 retired PLIBs tested were divided into 4 groups and represented by geometric coordinates (X, Y, Z), where X represents the capacity (Ah), Y represents the internal resistance (mΩ), and Z represents the voltage (V), as shown in Table 2 . The more batteries there are in a group, the higher the consistency of their electrochemical performance. Among them, the average capacity of the retired PLIBs is 20.16 Ah, indicating that the capacity retention rate of most the batteries are above 80 %.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The effect of high-temperature cycling for SOH
ce:section.ce:para: Battery SOH curve under different temperature as shown in Fig. 4 . In comparison with cycling at room temperature (25 °C), the battery SOH decreases rapidly when operating at high-temperature (55 °C). At room temperature, the battery SOH decreased from 87.08 % to 80.24 % after 400 cycles. Correspondingly, the battery SOH decreased from 86.16 % to 50.60 % during high-temperature cycling. Compared with room temperature cycling, the decay rate of battery SOH increased by 419.88 % after 400 cycles at high-temperature environment. In the first 100 cycles, the battery's SOH degradation is 2.96 %; after 200 cycles, the degradation speed of the battery's SOH accelerates, with each 100 cycles resulting in a 7.96 % reduction; after 300 cycles, the battery's SOH rapidly degrades, with SOH degradation reaching 19.04 %/100 cycles, as shown in Fig. 4(b). In the early stages, high-temperature accelerates the insertion and extraction of lithium ions from the electrode, accelerating the growth and rupture of the solid electrolyte interface (SEI) layer, leading to the degradation of electrochemical performance [34]. In the late stage, the volume change of graphite can reach 10 %, causing SEI layer rupture and lithium ion loss, accelerating the battery capacity degradation and aging process [35].

ce:section.ce:section-title: The effect of high-temperature for electrochemical performance
ce:section.ce:para: The internal resistance variation with SOC is shown in Fig. 5 . As the SOC decreases, the internal resistance gradually increases. When the SOC is at 0, the internal resistance is at its maximum. After cycling 200 times at room temperature and high-temperature, the battery's internal resistance increased from 2.97 mΩ to 4.20 mΩ and from 5.46 mΩ to 8.83 mΩ, respectively. The internal resistance significantly increased after high-temperature cycling. After 200 cycles, the battery enters a rapid decay stage, and the internal resistance quickly increases. When the SOC is at 0 and the working temperature is at 25 °C, after 400 cycles, the internal resistance increases from 3.81 mΩ to 4.59 mΩ, as shown in Fig. 5(a). Simultaneously, with a working temperature of 55 °C and after 400 cycles, the internal resistance increases to 10.27 mΩ. This is because high-temperature cycling accelerates the rate of electrochemical reactions, the active materials inside the battery are excessively activated, leading to battery swelling and deformation [22].

As shown in Fig. 6 , the charge and discharge voltage curve of the battery has two plateaus. The operating temperatures are 25 °C and 55 °C, respectively. After 400 cycles, the battery capacity decreased from 22.42 Ah to 22.31 Ah and from 22.16 Ah to 15.04 Ah, respectively. Compared to room temperature, a high-temperature environment accelerates the electrochemical performance degradation of the battery. Moreover, after high-temperature cycling, the two charging voltage plateaus gradually increased from 3.269-3.341 V to 3.275–3.344 V and from 3.346-3.397 V to 3.348–3.427 V, respectively. This might be due to the deposition of lithium ions on the graphite electrode during the high-temperature cycling process. However, a declining trend was observed in the discharge voltage plateaus, decreasing from 3.279-3.177 V to 3.252–3.169 V, and from 3.177-3.155 V to 3.169–3.130 V. The main reasons for the decline in electrochemical performance during high-temperature cycling are the destruction of the crystal structure of the active material and the loss of lithium ions [36].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Heat generation characteristic during high-temperature cycling
ce:section.ce:para: Batteries operating in high-temperature environments face the most dangerous situation of thermal runaway. Further research was conducted into the influence of rates and temperatures on the thermal safety of retired PLIBs. The study compared the heat generation characteristics of the batteries at room temperature and high-temperature environments under different rates, as shown in Fig. 7 . After 400 cycles at 25 °C, the maximum temperature/the maximum temperature difference for 1C and 3C increased to 31.85 °C/0.71 °C and 43.66 °C/2.91 °C, respectively, corresponding to the increases of 37.08 % and 309.86 %, as shown in Fig. 7(a) and (c). The experimental results show that when batteries cycle at higher rate, heat generated will greatly increase, and the uneven distribution of temperature within the battery will become more severe. Therefore, the electrochemical tests discussed later in this article are cycled at 3C.

With the increase of discharge rate, the maximum temperature of the battery and the maximum temperature difference increased significantly. The operating temperatures were 25 °C and 55 °C, and after 400 cycles, the maximum temperature for 3C increased from 40.91 °C to 43.66 °C and 39.86 °C–44.75 °C, respectively, corresponding to the increases of 6.72 % and 12.27 %. Correspondingly, the maximum temperature difference increased from 2.05 °C to 2.91 °C and 2.14 °C–3.03 °C, respectively, corresponding to the increases of 41.95 % and 41.59 %, as shown in Fig. 7(c) and (d). Furthermore, an interesting phenomenon can be observed from Fig. 7 (d), the maximum temperature and maximum temperature difference decrease after 400 cycles. This is because after 400 cycles at 55 °C, the battery's SOH was only 50.36 %, as shown in Fig. 4(b). The experimental results indicate that when batteries operate at high-temperature conditions, the unevenness of the internal temperature distribution within the battery is exacerbated. An uneven temperature distribution inside the battery can not only shorten its lifespan but also make it more prone to thermal runaway. Therefore, when retired PLIBs cycle at high-temperature and higher rate conditions, proper thermal management design must be implemented to ensure battery safety and reliability.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Aging mechanism during overcharge and over-discharge cycling
The effect of overcharge and over-discharge cycling for SOH
ce:section.ce:para: Battery SOH variations during normal cycles, overcharge cycles, and over-discharge cycles are illustrated in Fig. 8 . After 800 cycles of normal and over-discharge, the battery SOH decreased by only 6.84 % and 8.12 %, respectively, reaching an SOH of 79.24 % and 80.24 %, without any apparent acceleration phase. However, when the battery is subjected to overcharge cycles, it rapidly enters a phase of steep decline right from the start. After 200 cycles, the battery SOH dropped by 9.44 %; after 800 cycles, the SOH was only 67.52 %. Compared with the normal cycles, after 800 cycles of overcharging, the decay rate of battery SOH increased by 156.25 %. The experimental results indicate that overcharge cycles accelerate the degradation of the battery's electrochemical performance as compared to over-discharge cycles. During the charge and discharge cycles of lithium-ion batteries, lithium ions are repeatedly inserted into and extracted from the electrode. In the process of overcharging, an excessive amount of lithium ions is extracted from the LiFePO4 electrodes and inserted into the graphite electrode, which accelerates the deposition of lithium ions on the graphite electrode [37]. The deposition of lithium ions on the anode leads to a continuous thickening of the SEI film and loss of active material, subsequently causing a rapid decrease in battery capacity. Therefore, to extend the service life of retired PLIBs when repurposing them, it is advisable to avoid overcharging conditions as much as possible.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The effect of overcharge and over-discharge for electrochemical performance
ce:section.ce:para: The changes in battery internal resistance during normal cycles, overcharge cycles, and over-discharge cycles are shown in Fig. 9 . Initially, the changes in internal resistance are relatively small. When the SOC falls below 20 %, the internal resistance increases rapidly; SOC = 0 %, the internal resistance is at its maximum. As the discharge process nears completion, the battery's polarization process intensifies, and the polarization resistance increases. There is a significant increase in the internal resistance of 400–600 cycles of over-discharge cycles and 600–800 cycles of overcharge cycles, as shown in Fig. 9(b) and (c). The battery with a higher nominal capacity is more sensitive to overcharge and over-discharge conditions, which may result from its worse polarization and heat generation causing poor lithium and heat distribution [18]. After 800 cycles of normal, overcharge, and over-discharge, the internal resistance at SOC = 0 % increased from 3.81 mΩ to 5.95 mΩ, from 3.82 mΩ to 8.20 mΩ, and from 3.72 mΩ to 6.93 mΩ, respectively. The experimental results indicate that compared with normal cycles, both overcharge and over-discharge cycles accelerate the increase of internal resistance. And, overcharging cycles have a greater impact on the increase of internal resistance. It is mainly ascribed to the lithium loss, electrolyte loss and anode material loss during overcharge cycling [18,38]. Moreover, the graphite electrode is in a lithium-ion-rich state, which makes the charge transfer at the interface more difficult.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Heat generation behavior under overcharge and over-discharge cycles
ce:section.ce:para: Further research was conducted on the effects of overcharge and over-discharge cycles on the heat generation and thermal safety of retired PLIBs. Following normal cycles, overcharge cycles, and over-discharge cycles, constant current discharge tests were performed on the batteries while temperature data was collected. The results, as shown in Fig. 10 , indicate that after 800 cycles of normal, overcharge, and over-discharge, the maximum temperatures for 3C increased from 40.71 °C to 43.12 °C, 44.60 °C–47.34 °C, and 40.27 °C–45.31 °C, respectively. The maximum temperature differences increased from 2.05 °C to 2.83 °C, 2.16 °C–3.04 °C, and 2.23 °C–2.91 °C, respectively. Consistent with the battery SOH test results, the heat generation significantly increased during overcharge cycles, and the uneven distribution of temperature within the battery will become more severe. This phenomenon could be attributed to side reactions that occur during overcharge cycles, such as the oxidation and decomposition of the electrolyte, the growth of the SEI film, and the deposition of lithium ions [19]. Additionally, the bubbles produced from side reactions are trapped between the anode and cathode, hindering the battery's heat dissipation to the outside [20]. Furthermore, when the retired PLIBs cycles at high-temperature, higher rate, overcharge, over-discharge, and so on, in order to ensure stable operation within the optimal temperature range, improve their performance and service life, it is necessary to design an effective BTMS. At present, there are mainly four kinds of BTMS, including air medium, liquid medium, heat pipe and PCM medium [39]. Another useful approach is to propose a model to predict heat generation, battery temperature, voltage, and the possibility of thermal runaway while a lithium–ion battery is discharge–charge under various operating conditions [40]. Such a model can be used to design battery packs more resilient to thermal runaway or assess how a battery pack would perform under hazardous conditions.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Health status assessment under high-temperature cycling
ce:section.ce:para: Evaluating the SOH of retired PLIBs is beneficial for maximizing resource utilization. Further research was conducted on the evolution of battery health status after high-temperature cycling. The voltage-capacity curve was processed using the PDF, with results shown in Fig. 11 . Shifts in the four peaks within the PDF curve were observed. After high-temperature cycling, the most significant changes were seen in Peak B and Peak C, indicating that they were more affected by high-temperature environment. Initially, the peak values for Peak B and Peak C were (3.3479, 20.8421) and (3.2590, 21.4790), respectively, as shown in Fig. 11(a). After 400 cycles, the peak values gradually decreased to (3.3650, 12.1451) and (3.2343, 14.1041), respectively, as shown in Fig. 11(b). The specific changes in the peaks of the PDF curve after high-temperature cycling are detailed in Table 3 .

The battery health status is assessed based on the frequency of the most prominent peak regions in the PDF curve, calculating the peak frequency for the integrated voltage ranges of 20 mV, 50 mV, and 100 mV. As shown in Fig. 12 , during the high-temperature cycling, the assessment results of the battery health are as follows. The fitting results indicate that there is a positive linear relationship in different integral voltage ranges whether charging or discharging. During the charging process, when the integrated voltage ranges are 20 mV, 50 mV, and 100 mV, the fitted root mean squares are 0.9865, 0.9845, and 0.9883, respectively. During the discharging process, the fitted root mean squares values increase first and then decrease, being 0.9384, 0.9782, and 0.9502, respectively. The results indicate that during the charging process, the choice of integrated voltage range has a relatively small impact on the assessment result of the battery's health status. The discharging process is more sensitive to the selection of the integrated voltage range, and the health assessment results of the battery are more accurate when the integrated voltage is 50 mV.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Health status assessment under overcharge and over-discharge cycling
ce:section.ce:para: To further study the influence of overcharging and over-discharging cycles on the aging mechanism, the PDF was used to process the voltage-capacity data, and the changes in the peaks were quantitatively compared. The results are shown in Fig. 13 . During the charge process of overcharging cycles, the PDF curve has two peaks, namely Peak A and Peak B. Initially, the peak values of Peak A and Peak B were (3.2572, 5.5672) and (3.3403, 19.4960), respectively. After 800 cycles, the peak values gradually decreased to (3.2729, 4.6826) and (3.3566, 16.0829), respectively. Additionally, during the discharge process of overcharging cycles, the PDF curve also has two peaks, Peak C and Peak D. After 800 cycles, they decreased from (3.2580, 21.9145) to (3.2384, 16.9243) and from (3.1625, 5.2943) to (3.1486, 4.1062), as shown in Fig. 13(b). Similarly, in the charging process of over-discharge cycles, the PDF curve also exhibited two peaks, Peak A and Peak B. However, the change in Peak A was not significant, as shown in Fig. 13(c). Therefore, Peak A cannot be used as a health assessment factor for retired PLIBs. The specific changes in the peaks within the PDF curves for both overcharge cycles and over-discharge cycles are shown in Table 4 .

In short, the degradation of the electrochemical performance of batteries through overcharging cycles and over-discharging cycles is closely related to the changes in the peaks in the PDF curve, especially the Peak B during the charging process and the Peak C during the discharging process. Therefore, Peak B and Peak C are chosen to assess the health status of the battery. For overcharging cycles, the relationship between the battery health status and the frequency of the peak area is shown in Fig. 14 . The fitting results indicate that there is a positive linear relationship in different integral voltage ranges whether charging or discharging. During the overcharge cycles of charging process and discharging process, centered on Peak B and Peak C, respectively, when the integrated voltage ranges are 20 mV, 50 mV, and 100 mV, the root mean square of the correlation between the peak area frequency and battery health status are 0.9596/0.9804, 0.9912/0.9617, and 0.9609/0.9446, respectively.

Similarly, Peak B and Peak C were taken as the research objects to study the relationship between the battery health status and the regional peak frequency after over-discharge cycles, as shown in Fig. 15 . The health status of the battery is closely related to the changes in the peak during over-discharge cycles. During the charging process, when the integrated voltage range is respectively 20 mV, 50 mV, and 100 mV, the root mean square of the fitting between the peak frequency of the area and the health status of the battery are 0.9662, 0.9851, and 0.9758, respectively. Moreover, during the discharging process, the fitted root mean squares are 0.9550, 0.9834, and 0.9855, respectively. In summary, the fitting results indicate that there is a positive linear relationship in different integral voltage ranges, regardless of high temperature cycle, overcharge cycle, or over-discharge cycle. The assessment result of the battery health status based on the regional peak frequency during cycles is accurate.

This study investigates the electrochemical-thermal behaviors of retired PLIBs under different operating conditions. When the retired PLIBs cycles at high-temperature, higher rate, overcharge, over-discharge, and so on, in order to ensure stable operation within the optimal temperature range, improve their performance and service life, it is necessary to design an effective BTMS. The main conclusions are as follows.
(1)
Compared with room temperature cycling, the decay rate of battery health status methods increased by 419.88 % after 400 cycles at high-temperature environment. After 400 cycles at 25 °C, the maximum temperature/the maximum temperature difference for 1C and 3C increased to 31.85 °C/0.71 °C and 43.66 °C/2.91 °C, respectively, corresponding to the increases of 37.08 % and 309.86 %. The operating temperatures were 25 °C and 55 °C, and after 400 cycles, the maximum temperature difference for 3C increased from 2.05 °C to 2.91 °C and 2.14 °C–3.03 °C, respectively, corresponding to the increases of 41.95 % and 41.59 %.
(2)
After 800 cycles of normal, over-discharge and overcharge, the battery SOH was 79.24 %, 80.24 % and 67.52 %, respectively. Compared with the normal cycles, the decay rate of battery SOH increased by 156.25 %. After 800 cycles of normal, overcharge, and over-discharge, the maximum temperatures for 3C increased from 40.71 °C to 43.12 °C, 44.60 °C–47.34 °C, and 40.27 °C–45.31 °C, respectively. The maximum temperature differences increased from 2.05 °C to 2.83 °C, 2.16 °C–3.04 °C, and 2.23 °C–2.91 °C, respectively.
(3)
The battery health status under different operating conditions was evaluated. During the charging process, the integrated voltage range has a relatively small impact on the assessment result. However, the discharging process is more sensitive to the selection of the integrated voltage range. The results indicate that the proposed method for evaluating the health condition of retired PLIBs is effective.

Caisheng Li: Writing – original draft, Data curation, Conceptualization. Xianqing Liu: Writing – original draft, Software, Investigation, Data curation. Changhong Wang: Writing – review & editing, Resources, Project administration, Funding acquisition. Lisheng Ye: Visualization, Software, Investigation. Tingting Wu: Writing – review & editing, Resources, Project administration, Formal analysis. Zhixuan Liang: Writing – review & editing, Investigation, Formal analysis. Zejie Zhang: Writing – review & editing, Validation, Conceptualization. Ying Zeng: Supervision, Software, Investigation. Kaizhe Li: Software, Data curation.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare no conflict of interest.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors gratefully acknowledge the financial support for this research from the National Natural Science Foundation of China (52176063).
